exercises.name: The Monotone Breaker
exercises.rule: Every sentence shifts pitch or volume from the last.
exercises.why: Monotone reads as disengaged — variance is the signal of presence.
exercises.objective: Train automatic pitch and volume variation so every sentence sounds different from the one before it.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: pitch/volume variance across sentences is the signal. Flat monotone drags tone score.
exercises.coach_insight: Pick one thing to change at every period — go higher, lower, louder, or softer — and make the shift big enough that a listener could mark it. The trap: starting varied, then sliding back into your default register by sentence three.
common_failure_modes: Strong variation in the first two sentences, then a slide into one flat register for the rest
Varying volume only while pitch stays fixed, so consecutive sentences still blur together
Speeding up instead of shifting pitch — a pace change standing in for vocal variety
Saving all the energy for the final sentence and delivering the middle in monotone
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight sentence-to-sentence contrast sustained through the middle of the rep, not just the presence of variation somewhere in it.
exercises.retry_objective: Exaggerate the pitch or volume shift at every sentence boundary until no two consecutive sentences share a register.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate explanatory or update-style prompts with a clear listener (a team, a client, a new hire) where flat delivery is the natural failure mode. Topics should be familiar enough that all attention can go to varying pitch and volume sentence by sentence.

prompts.text: Walk me through what you did at work yesterday.
Explain how your morning routine usually goes.
Give me a status update on your top three projects.
Describe the last meeting you sat through.
Walk through the architecture of our auth system.
Explain how our pricing tiers are structured.
Run through last quarter's financial highlights.
Summarize the patient intake workflow end to end.
Describe how a deal moves through your sales pipeline.
Explain the curriculum sequence for an incoming cohort.
Walk through how your CI/CD pipeline is wired up.
Brief the team on this week's incident postmortem.
Read the quarterly compliance update out loud, top to bottom.
Deliver a five-minute technical onboarding to a new hire.
Narrate your entire weekly board report without notes.

exercises.name: Volume Dial
exercises.rule: Open quiet. Build to peak. Land soft.
exercises.why: Trains conscious dynamics — volume is a tool, not an accident.
exercises.objective: Train conscious volume dynamics — opening quiet, building to a peak, and landing soft.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the rep must open quiet, build to a peak, and land soft. Flat dynamics across the arc drag tone score; without prosody, judge whether the transcript's intensity builds to a clear peak and resolves.
exercises.coach_insight: Give yourself three gears: open at near-whisper intimacy, let each beat climb, and drop below your opening volume for the last line. The trap is peaking too early — if you're loud by sentence two, you have nowhere left to go.
common_failure_modes: Opening at full volume so the build has no headroom
A flat middle with no rising intensity, then a sudden jump right at the end
Peaking early and coasting downhill for the rest of the rep
Ending on the peak — no soft landing, the final line hits as hard as the climax
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the shape of the whole arc — where the peak lands and whether the close resolves quieter than the open — over any single loud or soft moment.
exercises.retry_objective: Rebuild the arc deliberately — start noticeably quieter, hit one clear peak, and drop the volume on the final sentence.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate narrative prompts with a named audience and emotional stakes (a wedding toast, an origin story, a keynote) whose story shape naturally supports a quiet open, a rising middle, and a soft close. The arc should be invited by the content, not forced onto it.

prompts.text: Tell the story of how you ended up in your current job.
Recap the most memorable trip you've ever taken.
Walk me through your favorite meal, start to finish.
Tell me about a hobby that has slowly taken over your life.
Pitch our company in 90 seconds.
Tell the origin story of your startup.
Describe a turning point in your career arc.
Walk a client through the case for our flagship product.
Tell the story of a deal that nearly fell apart and how it closed.
Recap a patient case where the diagnosis surprised everyone.
Tell the story of shipping your hardest feature.
Describe the day you realized your team had finally clicked.
Deliver a toast at your closest friend's wedding.
Eulogize a mentor in three minutes.
Give a keynote about why your industry has to change — now.

exercises.name: Warmth Switch
exercises.rule: Add audible warmth on every people-related sentence.
exercises.why: Warmth in voice is what makes people-talk land as people-talk.
exercises.objective: Train audible warmth so people-related content sounds engaged and invested rather than clinical or detached.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: audible warmth on every people-related sentence is the rule. Clinical or detached delivery drags tone score; in text, transactional phrasing about people implies the same failure.
exercises.coach_insight: When a person enters the sentence, let your voice change — slow slightly, soften your edges, and smile as you speak; listeners can hear a smile. The trap: describing people in the same voice you'd use for a spreadsheet.
common_failure_modes: Résumé mode — listing a person's skills and outputs with no audible investment
Warmth on the first person mentioned, then clinical delivery for everyone after
Generic praise words ('great', 'amazing') standing in for specific, felt detail
Rushing through the people content as if it were logistics
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the people-sentences specifically — whether delivery and word choice audibly shift when a human is the subject — rather than the overall pleasantness of the rep.
exercises.retry_objective: Deliver every people-related sentence as if the person were in the room — slower, softer, and personally invested.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate prompts about specific people (a teammate, a mentor, a patient, a recruit) with a stated listener who cares about them, so warmth is the register the situation demands. The emotional context must be explicit — introduction, farewell, gratitude, or comfort.

prompts.text: Tell me about your team.
Describe a coworker who quietly carries the team.
Tell me about someone who shaped how you work.
Talk about a friend you've kept since childhood.
Introduce your direct reports to a new exec joining the company.
Tell a customer about the engineer who built their feature.
Walk a recruit through what it's like to join your team.
Describe a nurse on your unit who patients ask for by name.
Talk about a student who completely changed how you teach.
Introduce your co-founder to a room of investors.
Tell me about the client relationship you're proudest of.
Describe your closest mentor and what they taught you.
Deliver a goodbye message to a teammate who's leaving the company.
Comfort a friend who just lost their parent.
Welcome a family whose loved one is starting hospice care.

exercises.name: Authority Voice
exercises.rule: Drop pitch on every claim of fact. No upward inflection.
exercises.why: Authority lives in the floor of your voice, not the ceiling.
exercises.objective: Train convicted delivery so every claim of fact lands on a low, steady vocal floor with no apologetics.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: tone score reflects the audible 'floor' under claims of fact. Upward inflection on claims drags tone.
exercises.coach_insight: Before each fact, take a small breath and let the sentence sit on your lowest comfortable pitch — set the last word down, don't toss it up. The trap: an 'I think' or a rising lilt that begs permission for your own facts.
common_failure_modes: Hedges welded to claims — 'I think', 'sort of', 'probably' — undercutting stated facts
Rising terminals on the key claims while throwaway sentences land fine
Apologetic framing before the fact ('this might be off, but...')
Rushing through the claims so nothing has weight, even without upspeak
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the delivery of the load-bearing claims — the sentences a skeptical audience would push back on — more than the connective tissue around them.
exercises.retry_objective: Strip every hedge and trailing ending — restate each claim of fact with dropped pitch and full commitment.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate high-stakes declarative prompts before a skeptical or senior audience (a board, a regulator, the press) where the speaker must assert or defend facts. The stakes must make apologetic delivery costly and conviction the clear target register.

prompts.text: State what you're best at, in one sentence, three times.
Tell me three things you know for certain about your craft.
Declare your role and what you own at the company.
List five facts about your product that you'd stake your career on.
Tell the board the rebrand is working.
Defend this quarter's hiring plan against pushback.
Tell the team we are not pivoting and explain why.
Brief the press on the recall — what happened, what's fixed.
Walk the audit committee through why our numbers are correct.
Deliver a diagnosis and treatment plan to the patient's family.
Explain to a skeptical client why your recommendation stands.
Tell engineering the architecture decision is final.
Address the all-hands the day after a public misstep.
Testify in front of a regulator about your firm's controls.
Tell a roomful of doctors why your trial data is conclusive.

exercises.name: Downward Landing
exercises.rule: End every statement with a falling intonation.
exercises.why: Rising terminals turn statements into questions. Land them flat or down.
exercises.objective: Train falling terminal intonation so every statement lands like a period, not a question.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: every statement must end with falling intonation. Rising terminals on claims of fact drag tone score.
exercises.coach_insight: Treat the last word of every sentence as a step down — physically lower your pitch on it, like putting the period in the air. The trap is list rhythm: reciting items lets upspeak creep onto every line ending without you noticing.
common_failure_modes: Upspeak on list items — each entry rising as if asking whether it's okay
Falling endings on easy sentences but rising ones wherever content gets uncertain
Trailing off instead of landing — endings that fade rather than fall
Run-on connectors ('and... so...') that never give a sentence an ending to land
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight sentence endings above everything else — especially list items and the final sentence, where rising or trailing terminals concentrate.
exercises.retry_objective: Land every sentence down — consciously drop pitch on the final word of each statement.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate factual recap or briefing prompts with a defined listener (an auditor, a new PM, an exec team) built from declarative, list-like statements, each a natural place to land the pitch down. Neutral-to-serious contexts work best so upspeak is an obvious mismatch.

prompts.text: Describe what your job actually involves day-to-day.
Tell me three things you did last weekend.
List the cities you've lived in and one detail about each.
State your name, your role, and what you're working on this week.
Summarize what your company does in five sentences.
Recap last week's standup decisions for someone who missed it.
Lay out the four pillars of your product strategy.
Describe your treatment protocol for a routine case.
Explain how you evaluate a new investment opportunity.
Outline the lesson plan you're running tomorrow.
Brief the new PM on the three workstreams under their area.
Recap the key terms of the contract you just signed.
Walk an outside auditor through how revenue recognition works here.
Deliver findings from a security incident to the exec team.
Read a list of layoff impacts to the company at an all-hands.

exercises.name: Curiosity Lift
exercises.rule: End each thought lifted — like you're still discovering it.
exercises.why: Curiosity tone invites the listener in; pre-decided tone shuts them out.
exercises.objective: Train an open, exploratory register that ends thoughts lifted — inviting the listener in instead of shutting them out.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: thoughts must end lifted and exploratory, as if still being discovered. Pre-decided, closed-off delivery drags tone score; in text, verdict-style phrasing where wondering language belongs implies the same failure.
exercises.coach_insight: Let each thought end floating upward and unresolved, like you're mid-discovery — an audible 'what if' rather than a closing gavel. The trap: performing curiosity on top of pre-baked conclusions delivered in verdict tone.
common_failure_modes: Verdict delivery — conclusions stated with closed, final intonation where wondering belongs
A scripted-sounding list of ideas with no audible discovery between them
A formulaic lift tacked onto the last word while the content stays pre-decided
Closing the rep with a tidy summary that shuts the exploration back down
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight whether the exploration sounds genuinely unfinished — open phrasing, questions raised and left live — over the polish or completeness of the ideas.
exercises.retry_objective: End each thought genuinely open — lift the final phrase and leave audible room for the idea to keep going.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate open-ended brainstorm or speculation prompts (unsolved problems, futures, half-formed ideas) with a collaborative audience — a teammate, a co-founder, a curious friend. The context must reward thinking out loud, not delivering conclusions.

prompts.text: What's a question you've been chewing on lately?
Talk about a book or idea that's stuck with you this month.
Wonder out loud about something in your field you don't yet understand.
Describe a hobby you keep meaning to start and why.
Brainstorm three new product ideas, thinking out loud.
Walk through a customer problem you don't yet have an answer for.
Talk through a system design tradeoff you're still weighing.
Speculate about where your industry will be in ten years.
Explore why a recent patient case didn't follow the textbook.
Think out loud about a market you've never invested in before.
Reflect on a student who learns nothing like you expected.
Open a brainstorm about how to fix your team's biggest blocker.
Riff on a creative direction for the next campaign — no commitments.
Explore a belief you used to hold that you're starting to doubt.
Speculate on what your competitor is about to do, and why.

exercises.name: Conviction Floor
exercises.rule: No rising terminals on any claim of fact.
exercises.why: Builds the audible floor under your beliefs — listeners hear you mean it.
exercises.objective: Train an audible floor under stated beliefs so listeners hear that the speaker means every claim.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: no rising terminals on any claim of fact. Hedging or apologetic phrasing (might, maybe, I guess) implies the same failure in text and drags tone score.
exercises.coach_insight: Plant every claim: slow slightly, drop your pitch, and stop cleanly at the period — silence after a claim reads as confidence. The trap: softening your boldest sentence with a 'maybe' or a small laugh right when it needs the most floor.
common_failure_modes: Hedge words on the core belief — 'I guess', 'kind of', 'maybe' — exactly where conviction is required
Self-deprecating asides or nervous qualifiers after a strong claim, taking it back
Rising terminals on the thesis sentence while trivial statements land flat
Over-explaining after the claim, diluting a firm statement into a ramble
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the one or two boldest claims in the rep — whether they stand unhedged and land down — more than the average delivery across all sentences.
exercises.retry_objective: Restate the belief without a single hedge, landing every claim flat or down.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate belief-staking prompts before a skeptical or resistant audience (a panel that disagrees, a team losing its favorite project, a longtime client) where the speaker defends a position they genuinely hold. The pushback context must make wavering delivery visibly costly.

prompts.text: Tell me one thing you believe that most of your peers don't.
State the principle that guides how you do your work.
Name the rule you refuse to break, and why.
Tell me the one piece of advice you give every new hire.
Why is product-market fit the only thing that matters in early stage?
Argue for the single metric your team should be judged on.
Make the case for shipping less, more carefully.
Defend a controversial clinical decision you'd make again.
Tell investors why this is the right fund, the right vintage, the right time.
Make the case that grading destroys real learning.
Tell the team why you're killing their favorite project.
Argue for a hiring bar that everyone on your team thinks is too high.
Defend your refusal to take a meeting that everyone says you should take.
Stake your position in front of a panel of people who disagree with you.
Tell a longtime client you're firing them, and why it's the right call.

exercises.name: Pivot Tone
exercises.rule: Signal topic changes with a tonal shift, not a filler word.
exercises.why: Tone shift handles transitions that 'so anyway' and 'okay' do clumsily.
exercises.objective: Train tonal shifts as the transition signal between topics, replacing filler-word pivots.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: topic changes must be signaled by a tonal shift, not a filler word. Transitions carried by 'so anyway' / 'okay' / 'moving on' drag tone score; with prosody, a flat register across topic boundaries drags it further.
exercises.coach_insight: At each topic boundary, pause a beat, then re-enter in a different gear — new pitch and energy matched to the new content's weight. The trap: reaching for 'so anyway' or 'okay, next' to do the work your voice should be doing.
common_failure_modes: Filler pivots — 'so anyway', 'okay', 'moving on' — carrying every transition
One unchanged register across all three topics regardless of their emotional weight
Marking the first transition tonally, then sliding through the later ones on filler
A tonal shift that mismatches the content — bright energy entering the bad-news segment
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the topic boundaries specifically — how each transition is executed — rather than the delivery quality within any single segment.
exercises.retry_objective: Cut the filler pivots and mark each topic boundary with a deliberate change in pitch, volume, or energy.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate multi-topic briefing prompts with a defined listener (a board, a team, a partner) covering exactly three segments of differing emotional weight — a win, a risk, a change — so each boundary demands a tonal shift. Name the segments explicitly in the prompt.

prompts.text: Tell me about your morning, your afternoon, and your evening.
Update me on the three things on your plate right now.
Cover what you ate, what you read, and what you watched yesterday.
Walk through your week: work, family, and personal goals.
Brief me on product, then on engineering, then on go-to-market.
Give updates on hiring, churn, and burn — in that order.
Cover three patients on your rounds today, each with a different status.
Move through last week's wins, this week's risks, and next week's focus.
Pitch the deal, then walk the diligence, then close on the ask.
Talk through a launch plan: messaging, channels, then measurement.
Cover three classroom incidents, each with a different tone.
Brief the team on the new feature, the deprecation, and the outage.
Move through good news, hard news, and the path forward.
Cover a celebration, a layoff, and a strategy reset in one all-hands.
Update the board on growth, on a lawsuit, and on a CFO departure.

exercises.name: Read the Room
exercises.rule: Match your tone to a stated audience cue.
exercises.why: Same words, different rooms — tone is how you actually adapt.
exercises.objective: Train register adaptation — matching tone to a stated audience while the content stays constant.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: tone must match the stated audience cue. A register mismatch — breezy delivery for a grieving friend, stiff formality for a close one — drags tone score even when the words are right.
exercises.coach_insight: Picture the listener before you speak and let their state set your dials — warmth, volume, formality, speed. The trap: one presentation voice for every room; if the board and a grieving friend get the same delivery, you read neither.
common_failure_modes: One default register regardless of the stated audience
Adapting the vocabulary but not the delivery — right words, wrong voice
In two-audience prompts, both versions sounding nearly identical
Overshooting the adaptation — cartoonish baby-talk for a child, stiff formality for an exec
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight audience fit over absolute polish — a technically smooth rep in the wrong register should score below a rougher one that matches the stated room.
exercises.retry_objective: Re-run the rep with the audience held vividly in mind, adjusting warmth, pace, and register to what that specific room needs.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt must name the audience and their emotional state explicitly (a skeptical board, a grieving friend, a curious eight-year-old), since the audience cue is the exercise itself. Favor two-audience variants that deliver the same content twice so the register shift is measurable.

prompts.text: Introduce yourself — to a new neighbor over the fence.
Describe your job — to a curious eight-year-old.
Explain what you do this weekend — to your best friend, then to your boss.
Tell the story of your day — to a partner who's had a hard one.
Deliver the same product update — to engineering, then to sales.
Pitch the company — to a skeptical board.
Share quarterly results — to a team that just lost a deal.
Explain the diagnosis — to a patient, then to a fellow physician.
Walk through the investment thesis — to an LP, then to a junior analyst.
Brief the same incident — to engineers, then to legal.
Give the same lesson — to a kindergarten class, then to graduate students.
Announce the reorg — to senior leaders, then to the broader team.
Deliver the same condolence — to a friend who's grieving, then to a colleague you barely know.
Tell a hard truth — to a mentor, then to a direct report.
Defend the same decision — to a friendly reporter, then to a hostile one.
